title: Why The Same Brand Is On The Bottom Of Every Boot

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Riddle me this: Why is this little yellow symbol on every single good boot?

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Because that is the most important slab of rubber in footwear history.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: The original version of this design is what conquered K2 for the first time. Jim Whittaker, the first American to summit Mount Everest, wearing this sole.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Suddenly, the elite mountain climbers at the time were flying up mountains because they had some rocket fuel under their feet, invented by one of the greatest climbers to ever climb, Vitale Bramani, aka Mr. Vibram. That's just a combination of his name.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Mr. Vibram saw something at the time that no one else saw. With rubber, we shouldn't be trying to copy the bottoms of old leather dress shoes, we should be trying to copy tanks.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: And that is exactly what this is. The Carrarmato, Italian for tank. The Carrarmato exists though because of what happens when I do this.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: I take these, run them under water, and then to simulate what actually happens, I'll take these and I'll put them in our freezer.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Tena's going to kill me if this freezer gets dirty.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: What I just simulated is thought to have killed six of Vitale Bramani's climbing companions on a climb that he was actually on. That was part of the recipe that led to their death. And of course, their deaths brought the Carrarmato to life.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: But not for nothing, that was 90 years ago. There weren't giant 70 billion dollar footwear brands 90 years ago. They are way bigger than Vibram.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: So, my question was, why is Vibram still relevant? Especially because they're still making the exact same outsole. Well, it did update in design a little bit, but pretty similar.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: I really wanted to find out, especially because Vibram is more of an engineering company than anything else. So, I just emailed them and said, can I come over? And they said, ask your mom. And I, my mom said yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: And on top of that, they let me come in, try everything out, annoy their lead designers, and say why do these lines spin around the boot? Why are they flat? What is this star doing in the center?

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: In the winter time, I used your soles to sprint across ice, now going up this very steep concrete thing with water coming down it. How are you doing that? How exactly are you doing that? I'd like to start my own outsole company.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: And yes, I did ask the big question. Nike is a 70 billion dollar company. There's other giant companies. Why do they all still come to Italy to you guys when they want the absolute best?

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: So, today's agenda is to go over three things: traction, grip, and what 70 billion dollars still can't buy.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: We are gathered here today to celebrate the life of Michael's Macy's branded Alfani underwear, which he has worn weekly for approximately 14 years.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Its death follows the tragic moment of Taylor's sister Cassidy trying to be nice when she visited and folding their laundry for them.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: As we all know, she folded Michael's undies and apparently she and her new boyfriend Jordan did discuss that it was time for Michael to get...

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: New underwear. Hello old chap. Good to see you.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Alfani?

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Ay, himself. Michael, before I go, who's going to carry my torch?

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: No, I don't want to replace you. Who could possibly ever replace you?

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Only one brand is worthy of caressing your butt and the thing on the front.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Sorry, I had a case of the giggles. What is that thing? Anyway, the brand goes by the name Unbound Merino. Their men's merino wool boxer briefs have 1,335 five-star reviews. They're made from superfine merino wool.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: What micron is the wool fiber?

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: What? Uh, 18.5.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Yeah, wow.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: It's more than you can ever need, and the dual structure of wool's fiber means it can absorb up to 30% of its weight in moisture and not feel wet. It's temperature regulating, and the chemical makeup of wool helps it naturally resist odors. Great for the upcoming summer weather.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: I mean Alfani, that's incredible, but how, the history we've had...

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Michael, please. Get some new underwear. You're a grown man. Macy's killed me for a reason. Check out the link in your own description and feel the unbound bounds of actually good underwear.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Okay.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: The two most important words in this video are grip and traction. Bramani and Vibram is the master at both. But in the 1930s, when Bramani was climbing, that was not the case.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: You had these. This was your grip. You probably recognize these if I do this. Do those look more familiar? They're espadrilles. What's very important about them is their sole. This is a wrapped rope sole made from jute.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Climbing footwear in the 1930s did not look like this, but it did use this sole. So, that's why I got them. Luckily these don't fit me so they look terrible. Get in there you little bugger.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: At the time, in the 1930s, rope soles were really good when you were going up smooth and dry rocks because they can get into all the little nooks and crannies on the rock. The flexibility of the soles combined with the kind of soft bouncy aspect of the rope and everything allowed climbers to get a lot of grip, or some grip.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Before visiting Vibram, I was grouping grip and traction into one thing. You can't do that. When you think of grip, think of to be the easiest way, think of sticky rubber on a very flat smooth surface. That is friction, that is locked down and you're gripping onto the ground. That is where these rope soles came in handy on a climb. But they suck for anything else.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Okay, now pretend I'm a boot. When we're talking about traction, we're talking about actually biting into the ground. And these are the teeth that were biting into the ground around this time period. Technically these should be hobnail boots, which I think I have a shot of from Limmer of, but these are 1940s French overboots.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: This is probably not what most mountain climbers were doing, but maybe some people kept them. You'd put these on and those iron spikes bite into the ground so you're locked in. That is traction.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Bramani was an elite level climber. Like I said, probably one of the best in the world. He was the personal tour guide for King... King Albert the first of Belgium when he was climbing the Dolomites. That just like shot into my brain like I was...

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: That's my King Albert the first of Belgium, nice to meet you.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Whoa, whoa, sorry. Did I do that thing where I get possessed by King Albert the first of Belgium again?

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Long story short, Bramani is an elite level climber and he belongs to a group of fellow elite level climbers called -

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: I had my friend Domi read it for me because she's Italian, she lives in Brescia.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Società Escursionisti Milanesi.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: But I'm just gonna call it SEM. The people in SEM also elite climbers.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: But recently, Bramani climbed a mountain called Punta Rasica and he was about to do it again.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: And 18 other people from SEM were like, 'I would also love to do that. Also, the weather is really nice, so let's just pack really light. We can go up and down super quick. We'll only bring our rope-soled shoes and we'll leave our ice axes behind and a lot of our packs behind.'

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: In short, of course, the weather changed very fast. All of a sudden, they were almost at the top and they had a huge problem.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: It was freezing cold, the rope-soled shoes were ineffective. A lot of climbers took off their rope-soled shoes and tried to descend just in their wool socks because they thought they had a better chance, but six people ended up dying.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Not just because of the footwear. It was a system-wide failure. Too many people moving too slowly with bad footwear and speed was the main thing here.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: But Bramani looked at footwear and said, 'There's a lot of stuff I can fix here. Namely, traction and grip should not be separate things.'

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: So the question becomes, sure, okay, traction makes sense, but this is specifically engineered for a maximum amount of traction. So why? How does it work?

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: And the answer to that is actually best shown through a Nike Air Force One because Nike said, 'Vibram, would you mind helping us fix something?'

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: First thing you need to do when designing an outsole is decide, what is that outsole for? This outsole is for the basketball court, so you need a lot of grip.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: So when you flip this little fish over, you expect to see a relatively flat outsole.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: If my hand is the court, you're trying to make sure you have as much contact with the ground as possible.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: But of course, the court isn't perfectly smooth. We do have very nice lacquered wood, but on top of that wood is dust, sweat, little debris and stuff like that.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: So you need to have some things on the bottom of the sole that are going to displace that dust and sweat and everything on the ground.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: So these sharp edges and these rows and everything can actually come in contact with the lacquer wood floor and not just dust.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: And then if you're a basketball player, you're doing a whole lot of this. You're running forward, you're stopping, you're going backwards, you're going side to side.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: So we need to make sure the actual shoe is designed like that. This is my favorite part of the video by the way.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: When we have a player going forward, we need these guys, these little edges looking for the ground to be able to grab on and allow you to go forward.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: But also since this is circular, we need these guys when you're going side to side so you don't just slip this way and go forward that way.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: So why is it a circle you may ask? Well, because when you're playing basketball, the biggest thing that you're probably doing is pivoting.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: When you pivot, those circles allow you to freely move. And look at this: the main pivot points, your heel and your toe, actually have a little pocket taken out.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: So when you're putting all the weight there, you have please...

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: has as little grip as possible but you have it everywhere else but these are not really worn on the basketball court anymore they're specifically worn around town outside stuff like that

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: So Nike brings up Vibram and says this is iconic we need to keep this general language but we really want to make an Air Force 1 that's way better on the streets of New York or in your town whatever

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: So how do you keep fundamentally the same circular design built to allow basketball players to pivot and stop people going to New York from twisting their ankle because their shoe pivots on them

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: The answer is you keep those circles and the design language but you break them up and add edges all the way around

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: So now if my hand is the ground instead of rotating smoothly it gets stuck in all these different places

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: This transformation is one of the reasons Vibram is so powerful and brands go to them translating this iconic design to pure modern day performance very hard to do

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: So if we break down the Carrarmato sole this is not made for the basketball courts this is to give you predictable footing on uneven and unpredictable terrain

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: First thing we have is the world's largest lug the heel if you're going downhill obviously this can grip and bite onto certain things make sure you don't slip

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: You'll notice that these lugs on the sides are very flat and they're also pitching at an angle all the way around

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: When you put down your foot you're getting bite from the outside edges in all directions

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: and they're very flat and wide as you go to the top because according to Vibram that gives you maximum surface area when you're making contact with the ground

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: But what the heck is going on with these four-sided stars that was my biggest question

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: The story goes that Vitale Bramani looked up when he was walking through the

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Galleria Vittorio Emanuele II

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: and that's where he saw the shape of the yellow Vibram logo but also the story goes or at least it's a bit more internal to Vibram he looked down and saw these tiles which of course boom right here easy answer

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: But this four-pointed star is actually incredibly important in Italian gothic architecture

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: It's called a

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: quadrilobo

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: which she kind of like puffed into the microphone when she did it so I asked her if she could do it again and she got a little annoyed but still did it

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: quadrilobo

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Thank you Domi anyways very famous shape has a lot of symbolism to it especially in the world of religion

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: but also it represents the four cardinal directions north south east and west when you're the first guy adding this much traction to an outsole and you think where do I want people to have a lot of traction you think in every major direction north south east and west so you just put that symbol right here for that reason

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: But

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: it's if you think about it for more than 10 seconds now in 2026 you're like well what if you did like a circle or this weird design with a dip in it you could make it a lot better and that's true

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: That's the final question of the video it's broken up into three parts first we have to do grip in compounds

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: because we have something called Megagrip and it allows me to go up this

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: That's one grip this is the other

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Traditionally to get a ton of grip you want a really soft outsole

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: You want like when you look at rock climbers, that rubber at the bottom insanely soft and it oozes and falls into all the cracks of the rock and gives them really rock solid grip. But it's not very durable and it doesn't, it yeah, it's just not durable.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: But of course you are Vibram and you have a crack team of engineers saying, well, it doesn't need to be totally soft exactly because we can play with one more setting: the actual springiness of the rubber.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: If we have a really springy rubber as you walk or run, it's gonna go boink, boink, boink, boink. Obviously not that much, the rubber itself is gonna be doing that. But if we dampen that so the rubber doesn't spring back, the energy goes somewhere else and it goes thump, thump, thump, thump, thump.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: So what we could do instead of making an incredibly soft rubber that's going to get ripped up is we can make a harder rubber with less of that boink boink boink bounce and that thump thump thump instead is going to give the runner or the wearer more grip.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: And we'll make lugs specifically that bite into the ground when you're running forward and when you're descending, we'll reverse them so that way when you're slowing down, you also have traction there. And all of a sudden people like me can run up a 45 degree angle wet piece of concrete with mega grip and feel fine and not slip because they're changing the actual springiness of the rubber and keeping it firm and durable so you can run hundreds of miles with it at the same time.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: And that leads us to why Nike doesn't compete with Vibram even though technically they could.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Last time I checked, the market cap of Nike was 67 billion dollars. So the natural question I had was if you're worth that much money, why not just cut Vibram out and make your own soles and make a ton more money doing that? And uh it's for a few reasons.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: The first reason is just that would be kind of stupid. The added margin or money that Nike can make by making their own soles is completely dwarfed by the complexity of running a giant company like Vibram. That is a huge web of people that you can just outsource. You have to connect with two people at Vibram instead of hire 10,000 people.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: But the other thing that I'm consistently learning over time is that money does not automatically mean success. You can't just keep throwing money at something and it's going to beat everybody. That doesn't work.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Vibram has a knowledge base fortress and unless you can scoop up everything that makes Vibram Vibram, which you virtually can't do, you will never compete with someone like Vibram who is making the best outsoles in basically every sector of the footwear industry for everybody.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: If you try to rival Vibram, you are going up against New Balance, La Sportiva, Nike... every single brand that puts Vibram on the bottom of their footwear is going directly against you and you'll never win.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: And unless your name is Vibram, the most valuable thing you bring to footwear is probably not the outsole. It could be the design, it could be your logo, it could be some new woven technology, but probably not this.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: A lot of what they do is work with insanely talented designers at giant brands, but they look a little bit closer and say, 'while that design is cool, it's not as effective as you think.' This is what I learned from this brand, this is what I learned from this brand. Let's figure out...

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: way to actually pull performance out of it. And the best example of this that I have and my favorite story from meeting all the people at Vibram is this.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: And I'm going to change the story a little bit so this brand doesn't get mad at me.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: But the brand was like, hey Vibram, we want to change something very important, a few things very important, and it's better, so just do it. And Vibram was like, no, that's, you do not want to do that, trust us, you don't want to do it. And the brand was like, oh yes, we do. So instead of arguing with them, Vibram goes to their massive testing facility and they set up a little test and film it for the brand. And they say, this is what you want to do.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: And they show a guy going up a similar ramp that I was going up in this shot and immediately upon putting his foot down, he falls forward. And then they go, and this is what we're proposing. Same dude, same testing conditions, goes up that ramp without a care in the world and doesn't slip. Doesn't even need his hands for guidance. And Vibram's just chilling in the corner and they're like, so yeah, let us know if you want us to make that change, we could do it no problem.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Ooh baby. So we end here. Me asking Vibram, isn't can't you make this like a billion times better? Why are you still using this? And essentially they were like, the original design was so good that it's extremely capable and able to be used in a ton of different scenarios today. But in order to remain the best, we have to break up all of our designs into hyper-specific uses when we can. So we've destroyed the Carrarmato model for pure mountaineering with weight, with grip, with traction, everything. But this compound, the grip, the structure, the traction is way better for other use cases and is really good for general use cases, so it still survives today with minor tweaks as the times go on. And big brands still call them because they actually weren't the king of the mountain 90 years ago, they were the king of understanding how a rubber outsole should actually work in different environments. And by fundamentally seeing outsoles a different way 90 years ago and never straying from that idea and becoming a boot company, there is nobody else in Vibram's league. Any brand, no matter how big, that makes this part of the boot or sneaker is going to lose to these guys.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Okay, anyways, that's about it. I'll see you soon. We're talking about Ventile next video.

highlights: The Vibram "Carrarmato" (meaning tank) sole was born from tragedy after founder Vitale Bramani lost six climbing companions to inadequate footwear, leading him to revolutionize mountain safety by combining grip and traction into a single rubber design.

A critical distinction exists between "grip," which utilizes friction and surface contact for stickiness on smooth surfaces, and "traction," which uses physical lugs or "teeth" to bite into unpredictable or uneven terrain.

Vibram achieves high performance by manipulating the dampening or "springiness" of rubber; by reducing the energy that causes the sole to bounce, they can use harder, more durable compounds that still provide elite-level grip.
The iconic four-pointed star in the center of the sole, known as a *quadrilobo*, was intentionally designed to provide reliable traction in all four cardinal directions: north, south, east, and west.

Despite their massive market caps, giant footwear brands continue to partner with Vibram because the company maintains a specialized "knowledge fortress" of engineering and performance testing that is nearly impossible to replicate or compete with.
summary:
This transcript explores the history, engineering, and enduring market dominance of Vibram, the Italian company world-renowned for its high-performance rubber outsoles. The story begins with founder Vitale Bramani, who was motivated to revolutionize footwear after a 1937 mountaineering tragedy where six of his companions died, partially due to inadequate rope-soled shoes. Bramani developed the *Carrarmato* (meaning "tank") sole, the first of its kind to combine traction and grip by mimicking tank treads. This design allowed climbers to "bite" into uneven terrain while maintaining friction on smooth surfaces, eventually becoming the gold standard for elite mountaineers and the foundation for the modern outdoor footwear industry.

A central theme of the discussion is Vibram’s identity as an engineering firm rather than just a rubber manufacturer. The transcript details how the company manipulates the physical properties of rubber—specifically balancing "springiness" and dampening—to create compounds like Megagrip that offer superior durability without sacrificing stickiness. Beyond mountaineering, Vibram collaborates with global giants like Nike to refine iconic designs, such as the Air Force 1, by applying geometric principles to enhance multi-directional traction. For example, the use of the *quadrilobo* (four-pointed star) in their designs serves both a symbolic and functional purpose, providing predictable footing in every cardinal direction.

Finally, the video addresses why multi-billion-dollar brands continue to outsource their outsoles to Vibram rather than developing their own. The author concludes that Vibram possesses a "knowledge base fortress" and specialized testing facilities that are nearly impossible to replicate. By focusing strictly on the interface between the shoe and the ground, Vibram has established itself as an essential partner to the entire footwear industry. Their success lies in a fundamental understanding of how rubber interacts with diverse environments, ensuring that even as technology advances, their 90-year-old original designs remains relevant through hyper-specific engineering and minor modern tweaks.

The Iron Snail is a men’s fashion vlog starring a young man named Michael and featuring a snail no bigger than a quarter. The two are set on taking over the world of fashion by creating a clothing line to end all clothing lines. Until then, we’re here to tell you EVERYTHING you need to know about the best clothing out there, from the highest quality raw denim jeans to the warmest jackets to the sturdiest boots…the Iron Snail has got you covered. (written by Taylor, though Michael is sometimes known to talk about himself in the third person… xoxo)
topics: The history and invention of the Vibram Carrarmato outsole
Technical differences between grip and traction in footwear
Engineering and design of high-performance rubber outsoles
Vibram's market dominance and partnerships with major brands
Properties and performance benefits of Merino wool apparel

The Iron Snail is a men’s fashion vlog starring a young man named Michael and featuring a snail no bigger than a quarter. The two are set on taking over the world of fashion by creating a clothing line to end all clothing lines. Until then, we’re here to tell you EVERYTHING you need to know about the best clothing out there, from the highest quality raw denim jeans to the warmest jackets to the sturdiest boots…the Iron Snail has got you covered. (written by Taylor, though Michael is sometimes known to talk about himself in the third person… xoxo)
